title: CollabWrite
content:
CollabWrite is a real-time collaborative document editing backend built to handle multiple users editing the same document simultaneously with minimal latency.

The core challenge was concurrent editing without conflicts. The backend uses a custom character-level CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) — each character gets a unique identity based on a site ID, counter, and timestamp. Two users can insert text at the same position at the exact same time, and both clients always end up with the same final document, no locking or rollback needed.

Real-time communication runs over FastAPI WebSockets with persistent connections per document session. Clients receive incremental updates the moment another user makes a change, with cursor positions and user presence tracked across sessions.

Database write pressure was a real problem — every keystroke hitting PostgreSQL directly would cause lock contention. Live document state is stored in Redis and flushed to PostgreSQL periodically through an async background loop. Moving document state to Redis makes the architecture a true distributed system. Multiple server instances can share and synchronize document state through a centralized store, enabling horizontal scaling without binding sessions or application state to a single server instance.

The system also supports AI-assisted editing like paraphrasing via Hugging Face models, with inference running in a thread-pool executor so it never blocks the WebSocket event loop.

Stack: FastAPI, WebSockets, PostgreSQL, Redis, Python, Hugging Face.

title: DocuMind
content: DocuMind is a production-oriented document Q&A platform built on a local-first LLM stack. It enables natural-language querying over uploaded PDFs with full auditability, grounded citations, and modular multi-agent orchestration via LangGraph. ## Core Architecture ### Multi-Agent Pipeline (LangGraph StateGraph) The system decomposes document Q&A into discrete, failure-isolated stages: query routing → hybrid retrieval → optional structured extraction → response synthesis → optional tool calls. Utilizing a LangGraph StateGraph with per-agent audit accumulation, single-stage failures do not cascade; each agent contributes to a shared state object and logs an independent audit entry, ensuring modular failure isolation. ### Hybrid Retrieval Engine To handle heterogeneous documents, complex user queries are decomposed into optimized sub-queries via a query planning step to bypass single-embedding bottlenecks. Ingestion uses PyMuPDF parsing with a recursive chunker to preserve semantic boundaries. The retrieval engine then executes a two-pronged strategy: - Dense retrieval: Semantic vector search over ChromaDB embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) utilizing page-level metadata for precise citation. - Sparse retrieval: BM25 keyword fallback to capture rare terms and exact phrases that embeddings miss. Results are merged, deduplicated on chunk overlap, and reranked with a Cross-Encoder for precision, meaningfully improving recall over vector-only RAG. ### Speech I/O Adapters Voice queries are transcribed using OpenAI Whisper for robust STT handling of accented or noisy audio. Responses are played back via a local HuggingFace SpeechT5 pipeline (SpeechT5ForTextToSpeech, SpeechT5HifiGan vocoder, and SpeechT5Processor), keeping the entire speech stack dependency-contained. ### Grounded Generation & Production Guardrails Retrieved chunks carry source metadata (filename, page number, chunk index). LLM prompts enforce strict citation guardrails, making answers traceable by design. Local-first inference via Ollama keeps sensitive documents off third-party servers, with Groq as an opt-in hosted fallback for cost/speed tradeoffs. For production debugging and compliance, the system records an inspectable, structured JSONL audit trail of every agent step.

title: MCP Financial Analyst
content: MCP Financial Analyst is an agentic, tool-driven financial analysis system that answers natural-language investment and technical-analysis questions with verifiable data and transparent tool traces. The system orchestrates lightweight MCP servers covering prices, technical indicators, news sentiment, fundamentals, and chart generation, synthesizing a structured markdown report linked directly to concrete tool data. The architecture utilizes a stateful, three-stage LangGraph pipeline separating reasoning (planning tool calls), acting (executing against live MCP endpoints), and synthesis (building the final report). Staging prevents hallucinations by ensuring tool results are in hand before generation, while isolating partial failures so a broken indicator call won't block price or sentiment results. To eliminate provider lock-in, a litellm ChatLiteLLMRouter blends Groq and Gemini with weighted shuffle routing for automated failover, cost control, and sub-second execution resilience. Tool integration is fully dynamic: every MCP server publishes a JSON schema from which the orchestrator constructs Pydantic-backed LangChain StructuredTools at runtime, allowing new data sources to scale without modifying core pipeline logic. For end-to-end provenance, an SSE-backed MCPClientManager running on an AsyncExitStack handles tool listing, invocation, argument normalization, and output compaction. Outputs are completely auditable by default, storing tool calls, results, and reasoning steps as first-class fields rendered via a Streamlit UI featuring inline chart rendering and expandable tool call panels.

title: E-Beat
content:
E-Beat is a production geospatial operations platform built for law enforcement field coordination. It started with winning a Goa Police hackathon — after the win, the team was brought on to actually build and ship the platform, which is now live and used in real operational environments.

The platform centralizes officer tracking, task assignment, field reporting, and administrative coordination into one backend system organized around spatial boundaries — districts, stations, and beat areas.

The geospatial layer uses PostGIS for hierarchical spatial modeling, with strict constraints ensuring tasks can only be assigned within valid jurisdictional boundaries — enforced at the data model level, not just in application logic.

Real-time officer location tracking was a hard infrastructure problem. High-frequency GPS updates from field devices would have overwhelmed a relational database. The solution was a hybrid architecture: PostgreSQL and PostGIS handle structured operational data, while Firebase Realtime Database handles the live location stream — keeping the map responsive without polluting the operational database.

The platform also includes a staged approval workflow where field-submitted spatial records land in a staging table first and are promoted to production only after supervisor approval, preventing bad data from affecting live operations.

Stack: Django, PostgreSQL, PostGIS, Firebase, AWS. Shipped into production, actively used in live law enforcement operations.

title: Workforce and Event Management Platform
content:
A production workforce and event management platform built as a freelance engagement to replace fragmented manual workflows with a centralized backend system. It handles employee coordination, event assignments, attendance tracking, and reporting — actively used in live business operations.

Long-running tasks like report generation and bulk exports are offloaded to Celery workers backed by Redis, running completely outside the request cycle so users aren't blocked waiting for heavy jobs.

A key engineering problem was memory exhaustion during large exports — reports with thousands of records were crashing workers. The fix was streamed in-memory processing using BytesIO and sequential archive generation, building exports incrementally instead of loading everything at once. This made large exports stable and reliable.

The platform uses Django Channels with a Redis channel layer for real-time WebSocket connections from attendance hardware and live dashboards.

Deployments run on AWS with a Blue-Green pipeline using GitHub Actions, ECR, CodeDeploy, and ECS on EC2 behind an Application Load Balancer — zero-downtime releases with fast rollback via traffic cutover.

Stack: Django, PostgreSQL, AWS, Celery, Redis, Django Channels. Shipped into production, used in live business operations.

title: QRGuard: AI-Powered QR Security Scanner
content:
QRGuard is a real-time threat detection system designed to protect users from malicious links embedded in QR codes. It bridges the gap between raw threat intelligence and user-friendly security advice by combining traditional URL reputation checks with LLM-based semantic analysis.

The core technical challenge was translating fragmented security metrics from multiple threat engines into actionable verdicts. The system decodes QR data using `pyzbar` and cross-references extracted URLs with the VirusTotal API. To provide more than just a binary 'safe/unsafe' output, I integrated Google Gemini to perform real-time reasoning over VirusTotal’s analysis stats, generating human-readable verdicts (SAFE, WARNING, or DANGER) that explain *why* a link might be suspicious.

For persistence, I chose Apache Cassandra as the audit logging backend. While a simple SQL database could suffice for small scales, Cassandra provides a distributed, high-availability architecture capable of handling write-heavy scan logs with minimal latency, ensuring the system remains responsive even as the audit trail grows.

The entire ecosystem is containerized via Docker and Docker Compose, managing the complex system-level dependencies required for image processing (libzbar) while orchestrating the communication between the Streamlit frontend and the distributed NoSQL cluster.

Stack: Streamlit, Apache Cassandra, Google Gemini, VirusTotal API, Python, Docker.

title: Portfolio Backend & AI Chatbot
content:
A FastAPI portfolio backend and agentic chatbot serving as an interactive resume, delivering citation-backed, non-hallucinatory answers about my engineering background.To guarantee low latency and resilience, I implemented a dual-LLM routing pipeline. It dynamically routes queries to Groq (using high-speed Qwen models) for sub-second execution, falling back to Google Gemini if rate limits are hit or failures occur.A key engineering challenge was context drift in multi-turn conversations. To resolve this, I built a stateful, multi-stage pipeline: incoming queries pass through scope-checking guardrails, chat history is condensed into a search query, and an FAQ database is checked first to avoid unnecessary LLM calls.For retrieval, the system uses a hybrid approach. It integrates a semantic vector search (leveraging Hugging Face's 384-dimensional all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings via sqlite-vec) with SQLite FTS5 lexical keyword search. I coupled this with a custom SQLite-backed Knowledge Graph that extracts query entities and boosts related documents by 15%, injecting structured relational facts directly into the prompt.

Stack: FastAPI, SQLite, sqlite-vec, Groq, Google Gemini, Hugging Face, Python.

title: Professional Background
content:
Abhinav Kurup is a software engineer with 2 years of experience in backend engineering and applied AI. He has shipped 2 backend systems into production that are actively used in live operational environments.

On the backend side, he has built APIs using Django and FastAPI, worked with PostgreSQL (including geospatial systems with PostGIS), and used async patterns, WebSockets, task queues (Celery + Redis), and AWS infrastructure. He holds an AWS cloud certification.

On the AI side, he has built RAG pipelines, agentic workflows with LangGraph, and document intelligence systems — working with ChromaDB, Hugging Face models, and LLM-based systems focused on retrieval quality and grounded generation.

He is currently targeting backend and AI engineering roles in teams building intelligent systems, LLM-powered products, and production-grade backend infrastructure.

title: Educational Background
content: Abhinav Kurup completed his Bachelor of Engineering in Computer Engineering from Goa College of Engineering (2020–2024) with a CGPA of 8.55. Before that, he completed Class XII under CBSE at Kendriya Vidyalaya No. 1, scoring 94.2%.
